speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking with Dr. Zev Kohn in New York City, and this is tape number 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So now we're starting a, a new segment in, in this saga of Second World War. And I would like to go backwards now into Warsaw into the broader aspects of what was happening before the war in Warsaw, even though I was nine years old at that time. What was happening at that time, that there was virulent anti-Semitism that was even clear to me at the time. I don't know what was the occasion for the Poles, but I think that was the part at the time when the Germans walk-- walked into Czechoslovakia, and the Poles were very happy that happened. Incidentally, that's when Poland took a piece of Czechoslovakia too. And they were very happy. And the way they showed their happiness, they were throwing the Jews out of the windows in the university and beating up Jews on the streets. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From what I remember, one of the friends, a doctor of my father, was brutalized. Then there were pogroms, too. There were pogroms with the Jewish self defense. The Jewish porters, the shtarkers, organized themselves. And from what I understand, my father was, was working with this sort of counter or self defense in the sense that when-- if these porters would be beaten up by the Endeks. Endeks was a group of f-- rightist anti-Semitic movement in the university, and they were then beating up the Jews on the streets. But the porters-- the Jewish porters-- apparently were also luring them into gates and beating the shit out of them also, so that maybe went both ways. Anyway, I do know from my father that he was-- that he was enlisted then to provide medical help to-- in case of some injuries to the Polish-- I mean, to the Jewish porters hit by the Poles. I don't think it came, actually, to any kind of real serious loss of life or, or serious injuries. But it was going on, and I as a kid was already aware. Anyway we are now in 1940 in Rowne, East Poland. And some time around that time, maybe 1940, the Russians put the trains on the-- on the railroad station. And we knew that they are going to make searches house to house and pick up people that cross the border illegally or whatever and ship them at night to Siberia. I think that somebody tipped off my father because he had special papers saying that he's, as a physician, indispensable in town. They came to our apartment in the middle of the night at, at 2:00 or so and told us [RUSSIAN], which means pick, pick up your belongings, and we're going to ship you into Siberia, to the Russian paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then I remember that scene, and my uncle, incidentally, and my cousin were hidden in the attic-- my uncle, Shimon, and my cousin, Jerrzyk. And so when they-- when the guy came to ship us, my father showed him the papers and they relented, and that was the end of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I am coming to June 22, '41 when Germans invaded Russia, and the war broke out. For some crazy reason, because I had crooked teeth, my, my, my parents sent me with my sister to my aunt that lived at that's time in Lwow for some tooth correction or whatever. And sure enough, that was the time when the war broke out. We went with my sister to the railroad station to seek trains going back to Rowne to join our parents. And as we got to the railroad station, kid as I was, 11 years old, I decided to have soda. So I left the building where we were waiting for the train and went outside to have my soda. But what happened in the meantime, there, there came literally dozens, if not hundreds in my mind, of German planes. And they started bombing the railroad station exactly where I was buying the soda. And there was horrendous panic. Everybody started running. And I ran back to the place where I left my sister. And I remember this vividly. In the middle of this huge hall, my sister was standing. The hall was totally empty. There was nobody already there because the bombs started falling, and she was waiting there for me, obviously, putting, [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS] putting her own life online. But she was standing there alone and waiting. And I ran in, and, and at the moment I walked in, we just ran and hid ourselves in a basement of some house near the railroad station. And the bombs started coming and by the time we walked out from the basement-- the railroad station-- forget about this. It just wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the question was how to get out from this city and the Germans were coming. So we went to our aunt and through the employment of my uncle, they got a truck to transport the-- his family out of the city. And we walked to my aunt, and my aunt told the, the people that were guarding, checking the documents going on the truck, she says, this is my family. And through the-- through the intervention oh my aunt, Stepha, we, we got on the truck, and then we came to Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Rowne, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and either the same day or the next day, the Germans started-- as they advanced, they started bombing Rowne. My father was, as a physician, taken into the hospital. He was working in the clinic, but he realized he should go to the hospital, work for a hospital so if they evacuate, they will take him-- take him with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans started bombing the city. My father was in the hospital, and my mother took my sister and me out of this building, which was very shaky building-- old, five-stories building or so-- because we were afraid the whole building would collapse. Anyway, the bombs did fall on the front yard of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the bombing stopped, and we came back to the house. And there is a note, one of the hundreds of notes that my father, I think, printed and left around saying, please [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] join me in the hospital, giving the address where-- from where the evacuation of the families of the doctors from the hospital takes place. We immediately went there. My father was there. We got on the bus, and we left going deep inside Russia. There was about six, seven families, Jewish-- families of Jewish doctors. We all stuck together through the coming years. We helped each other. So we got on the bus, and by that same night, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the city was burning. And I think a day or two later, the Germans came in. And within two or three months, my aunt, Franka, that received us when we fled from Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her husband and her younger son that has the same name as I have, were all murdered by the death squads. [CRYING] Anyway, so we're on our way. One stop was in Zhytomyr, then the next stop was in Kiev. By the time we got to Kiev, Kiev was already empty. In other words, there was nobody on the streets, and from what I realized late-- later, the Germans had already-- they had their pincer movement around Kiev, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and that's where they trapped out all these Soviet armies. Anyway, we got out. We were then on the train, again, living on the train, and we ended up not far from Stalingrad on one of the tributaries of Volga. We stayed there through the summer. The life was very quiet there. It was a little village. But every day-- every day, anybody that wanted to hear the news realized that the Germans are cutting Stalingrad off from the north. That is to say that the-- that the link-- the railroad link, link with the-- inside Russia will be cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we packed up these families, as I said, whatever number it was, five, six, or seven. And we went to Stalingrad. There was a mob of people. There were thousands and thousands of people standing on the pier waiting for a ship [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on Volga to go south to Astrakhan because there were still trains leaving Astrakhan. Presumably, the day before the pier collapsed because it couldn't take the weight of all the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, the, the ship came. And I remember that I ran inside, and I found there was a mob of people. And of course, always the danger of me being suffocated by the mob. And the kid as I was, I ran, and I hid myself on top of the big pile of, of sugar bags because they transporting in the hull of the ship sugar sa-- sacks of sugar. And we got to Astrakhan. We got to Astrakhan. We started counting who, who made it to Astrakhan from that group. Turned out that some people couldn't make to the ship. They were either trapped or whatever, so we spent another couple of days, and they did come with the next ship. And then we got on the trains, and we went deep inside Russia. We went to Altayskiy Kray. The name of the place is Barnaul. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The name of the place is Barnaul. As we were passing Novosibirsk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got a fever. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, there was-- there were lice on the train. You couldn't move your bowels because the trains were moving all the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then the question was what fever I got. I don't know how much it was, but it probably was like 105 or so fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me off the train in Barnaul and put me in the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had violent diarrhea too. Anyway, turned out later that I had severe Streptococcal infection. But my mother, I don't know in what way they got information that the hospital where my father was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] will be passing through Barnaul. So both my sister and my mother were standing-- mind you this is Siberia. It is 40 below zero centigrade. They were standing on the-- on the railroad station and waiting for that transport of my father's hospital to go by. All they knew is the number of the hospital. And sure enough, one of these nights that we were waiting, the transport came and my father was on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take him much-- very long. He just got off the train, told everybody-- told nobody nothing, just walked off the train. Under the circumstance like this during the war, they could have shot him on the spot for desertion. But next morning, he went to another hospital, and they were happy to have him in the other hospital, so they didn't report him. He started working at new hospital. Then the first thing he did, he went and he stole some sulfur medicine. And in the middle of the night, somebody came and gave me sulfur-- first sulfur ever in Russian maybe-- and that saved my life. I got better, and I came back to the-- to the house. I was going to school. Then one day my father doesn't come back from work. He was to come 5:00, 6:00 in the evening. He didn't-- he didn't show up. And few hours later, KGB comes [INAUDIBLE]. They searched the house, and whatever it was, somebody didn't like my father, whatever it was. They put my father behind bars. They searched the house, and we're living in a peasant's cottage, but a good peasant cottage because it was made by the son of the-- of the lady that had the cottage, so it was very insulated, and we didn't suffer from, from cold. But the deal was that my father, being a doctor, he, he will obtain-- he will obtain rations for wood that will permit to heat the cottage. And of course, my father was now in jail, so it became difficult, but my mother still used the old permit that also was terribly risky. And she got some wood, and we were staying there. In the meantime, my mother was donating blood for few hundred rubles to buy-- to buy some food for my father and send it to jail to give him to in jail. Afterwards when my father finally got out from the jail, he was saying that this food wasted even though my mother was donating blood and she was starving herself. We have nothing to eat. We. we. we waited in line for bread, 400 grams, and sometimes bread wouldn't arrive, so we wouldn't eat anything all day long. But anyway she was giving the blood to buy him that food that went into jail that he never ate because there were 60 people in a room the size of maybe 20 by 20 feet lined up in layers-- four layers of bunks, four levels, and they would divide everything. So when you divide a little bit of milk or frozen milk or whatever between 60 people or whatever, then nothing came, came to him. Anyway, then another miracle happened. At that time, there was some agreement between Churchill and Stalin and they decided to organize a Polish army in Russia out of all these prisoners of war that Russians took in '39, I mean, these that were not shot in Katyn. And there were many of them because they basically were shooting only the, the officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as a Polish citizen [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during one of these night sessions with KGB, incidentally, there was a little glimpse of hope there too because the guy that was, well, you in America call it interviewing, but it wasn't exactly interviewing. It was-- it wasn't torturing either. But it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pressuring or-- the word will come to me. It turned out from KGB-- turned out that, that he gave a hint to my father that he is a Jew, and he will try to help my father. So then when he started pushing my father, both physically and verbally, my father says, take it easy. He says, I'm a Polish citizen. And that apparently gave the other guy a clue or whatever that they have to free my father according to the new agreement with the British. So they let go of my father. And by that time, he couldn't walk because he, he could not-- he didn't walk for three or four months, the time that he was in jail, so his knees were stiff. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked terribly decrepit, so I went with him to the barber shop. He needed a shave and a haircut, and he still had his old uniform from the hospital even though underneath he had civilian, civilian clothing. He went for a haircut, and from there, he went to officer's club to, to get some bread with the same uniform-- with the same coat on top without uniform underneath. Obviously, had they discovered that he wasn't working for the Russians anymore in any way, they would have probably sent him back to Siberia for good now, never seen the daylight, but they didn't. And that was the first time in good many month that we went to, to, to a place where I really had a lot of bread. I don't know whether I had enough of bread, but I wasn't hungry anymore. I also was getting some kind of a food quote, unquote 'supplement' from a friend-- a colleague of mine that was an orphan sitting next to me in the classroom. And he was giving me [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] are the pressings from, from sunflower seeds. The oil usually goes to humans, and the, the leftover from these pressings is usually given to the animals, to the horses for, for their consumption.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this kid was stealing the [NON-ENGLISH] from some horse. He found some stable. And so he was steeling enough for himself. He was bringing it to school. That's what kept me going for quite awhile because otherwise, there was really days there was nothing to eat. So then once my father was out of jail, he got his hair cut. We had the first loaf of bread. We gave a little bit of vodka, little bottle, to the train station commandant, and he put us on train because my father said that if I don't go-- don't get the hell out of here in a hurry, they may change their mind. They will lock me up again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes they were locking and sometimes they were also, apparently, shooting even though I don't know whether it can be substantiated historically because apparently, they did not shoot at the time. They were just sending to fr-- to the front. But anyway, we got out. We got on the train, and we came to, to south of Russia. To be precise, it was near Tashkent. The name of the place was Guzar. It's not that exactly it was a place because it was a place in the middle of the nowhere. It was a place in the middle of a desert, not sand desert, but windswept, dry desert. And that is where my father came to enlist in the Polish army that was then organizing to leave Russia through the Caspian Sea and go to Persia and then fight the war against the Germans in Italy. The only problem was that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the fucking Poles didn't forget their feelings about the Jews, and that was a handicap.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were not taking Jews. They were not taking Jews into the Polish army. They were flatly denying the-- any kind of admission. The soldiers, I mean the enlisted men, they certainly didn't take. So then it came to it that my father came up with a true story that in 1921, towards the end of the First World War, my father was enlisted in the then new Polish army to fight the Bolsheviks because he was just a medical student, and he was then running some transport train. And he told it to these Polish officers in south of Russia and they took him into the Polish army. And I think I'll finish this tape now. No? Yes? We have a few more minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. OK. So then what happened, we were sitting in this little place in the middle of the nowhere starving. We lived in one tent I would say 20 by 20 feet, about four Jewish families-- again, families of Jewish doctors. And, and we're all dying from disease and from starvation. My father had typhoid fever. His grand total weight at that time, I think-- I think was maybe 40 kilograms, which is less than 80 pounds. My mother had jaundice and massively swollen legs from starvation. I had bloody diarrhea to the extent that I could never get from the tent to the hole in the ground, which was about 50 feet away. And then I must have stepped on something because where-- I was walking barefooted, and I developed a big abscess on the base of my right toe and which was, obviously, quite seriously looking. There was no place to go to open it. My father then was out of the hospital already. He says I will go-- going to open it myself, and he did. He cooked a pair of scissors for cutting nails and just poked a hole. Puss came out, and I felt better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the end of this tape. OK.
